content.problemStatement: There are **N** sets of integers from 1 to **K** both inclusive. Find out number of pairs of sets whose union contains all the **K** elements.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and K. The next N lines contain an integer len_i denoting the number of elements in the i-th set, followed by len_i space-separated integers in the range [1, K] denoting the elements in the set.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer corresponding to the number of pairs of sets whose union contains all K elements.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example 1. No pair of sets is there with its union = 2. For example 2 and 3, all the pairs are valid.
Explanation: The pair of sets {1, 2} and {3} has a union of {1, 2, 3}, which contains all 3 elements.
Explanation: The pairs of sets {1, 2} and {3, 4}, and {1, 2} and {1} have unions that contain all 4 elements.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can iterate over all pairs of sets and check if their union contains all K elements. We can use a bitmask or a set data structure to efficiently compute the union of two sets. The time complexity of this approach is O(T * N^2 * K), where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of sets, and K is the number of elements in the universe.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2 * K) — The time complexity is dominated by the iteration over all pairs of sets and the computation of their union.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * K) — The space complexity is dominated by the storage of the sets and their unions.
